comment.body:
Alma, I’ve got several questions pertaining to the math behind your analysis. 

How are you calculating whether positioning is a short/long fly pattern? Is it just an aggregation of MM GEX exposure? 

How do you calculate things like where a centroid is or where pivots are occurring? I know it’s just looking at the data of what the overall market is betting on but how do you do that at least mathematically? 

How do you conceptually or mathematically calculate how all of the interactions between the options positioning Greeks impact hedge my dynamics and therefore market drift to come to the conclusions you do of where a floor or ceiling is put in for prices? And the timing of it too (incredible btw) 

I’m not necessarily looking for exact equations or exact weightings/parameters but a direction or concepts to study and then run with from there. Similar to how you gave us modern methods and concepts of how you have certain models built but not exact parameters and weightings. 

Thank you, and I hope you have a great weekend

comment.body:
this shot/long fly is based on the speed profile, netted out. 
positive speed convexity = long fly
negative speed convexity = short fly.

speed has a time decay, just like gamma
for this time of analysis you really need the 3rd/4th order derivatives, and tons of datas.

the centroid is literally the centroid of the profile. thats the area or line where the upside and downside gamma effects affect the spot equally, and the intraday dealer hedging flow leaves 50/50 % chance for each direction. time decays are taken into account when calculating it. this is my propriotary equation.

for your Q, there is no short answer. read Nassim Taleb's book of "dynamic hedging" first.
you will find a lot of answers for that .the rest is creativity

comment.body:
no answer for that. literally.
delta neutral strats have positive expected value at the end, but concave payoff. 
it is like being short gamma. 
plus, the delta neutral strats are using temporal market anomalies, that go away after a while. they require dynamic approach, constant retesting.

l/s strat if taken in the long-term is also good, but personally i don't do it.
Warren Buffet proved what is the best strat to beat the market.

being long vol/momentum trader is also good if a person does it clever, because the strat has mathematically a positive skewness, and convexity in terms of payoff. time series trend following momentum i mean.. cross-sectional has crash-risk, fat left-tail.

comment.body:
so delta neutral short vol strats have concave payoff, but net positive expected value,
trend following momentum, long vol strats have convex payoff, but mathematically net negative expected value.

doing both, being long vol and short vol, hedges out a lot of risk.
next education post will tell this

comment.body: Alma, quick question, would you buy to hold for the next 2+ years if the correction is mild (less than 2-3%) during this window of risk? Or another way of asking, would you buy regardless once the risk window closes, given the bullish outlook

comment.body: I think Alma wrote somewhere that the next window of risk is late November. While time correction is a serious possibility it doesn't wash out the risk completely -- the accumulated imbalances must be cleared. I think sooner or later we get a correction, but the catalyst is unlikely to be what we could easily predict.
